text: Excuse me, can you help me?
David, can I ask you something?
Can you send me the report?
Good morning, Paula. You look busy.
I am. Can I ask you something?
Ricardo sends the report on Thursday. Can you explain the numbers to me before the call?
I can explain them now. Do you have ten minutes?
I do. And can I join your call with Miami on Friday?
You can, and you should. The account is yours. Can you print the report for it?
I can print it on Thursday. Sorry, could you repeat the day of the call?
Friday, at two. And Paula, you don't need the sorry.
I said: you don't need the sorry.
I can't sign it without the report.

Hi, Paula. David here. One thing before your call with Ricardo, and it is small, but it changes everything. There is one word in this office that opens every door, and you already use it. You just don't know its name. The word is can. Listen: Can you help me? Can I ask you something? Can you repeat that? Three questions, three different jobs, one word. Here is what it does. First, it says what you are able to do: I can explain the numbers, I can handle the account. Second, it asks for permission: Can I join your call on Friday? Third, and this is the one you need this week: it asks for things. Can you send me the report? Can you print two copies? Now, one warning, and it is the only mistake I want you to avoid today. The verb after can is always naked. Can help. Can send. Never can to help. In your language the verb has a little tail, and the tail wants to come with you. Leave it at home. And one more thing, because I know you are polite: could is just can in a suit. Can you explain that? That is a colleague. Could you explain that? That is a client. Both are correct, and nobody in the history of this company has ever been offended by either one. See you at nine.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. We have the call with the Miami client on Friday, and I want to tell you one thing before it, because I know you are nervous. The client speaks fast. Everybody in Miami speaks fast. It is not you. Here is what I do, and I am the manager: I say, could you repeat that, please? I say it two or three times in every call. Nobody has ever said no. Nobody has ever thought I am bad at my job. Think about it. When a client asks you to repeat something in Portuguese, do you think that person is stupid? No. You repeat it, and the call continues, and nobody remembers it one hour later. It is exactly the same in English. Now the real reason I am calling. I have watched you work for two months. You can handle the account. You can explain the numbers better than I can. You can talk to any broker in this country. There is only one thing you can't do yet, Paula, and it is not English. You can't ask for help without saying sorry first. Stop apologizing. Could you repeat that is a complete sentence. It does not need a sorry in front of it. See you on Friday.
Can you explain the numbers to the client?
I can't sign it without the report.
Could you print two copies for Thursday?
Can I join your call with Miami on Friday?
If Friday is difficult, I can wait until Monday.
I can't do Friday. Can we move the meeting to Monday?
Sofia can handle the account too.
Can you send me the report?
I can explain the numbers to the client.
